<!-- Generated by camerata. 31 principle(s). Edit principle sources, not this file. -->

### ARCH-PARALLEL-INDEPENDENT-1 — Independent concurrent calls in any code path run in parallel, not sequentially
Wherever code awaits two or more calls that have no data dependency on each other (independent repository lookups in a service, independent backend fetches in a UI component, independent third-party API calls, independent cache fetches), the calls run concurrently via the language's parallel-await primitive (try_join, Promise.all, asyncio.gather, errgroup, or equivalent). Sequential awaits on calls without a dependency are a violation. Real exceptions: calls with a true data dependency, calls that must be serialized inside the same transaction, and writes whose ordering matters semantically.

### ORCH-AUTOCALLS-LEDGER-1 — Auto-calls ledger + periodic judgment review
Every self-made clear-winner call is logged with date, rule, decision, alternatives, and why. The agent surfaces the accumulated batch for human accept/modify/reject on a weekly cadence. Further autonomous runs hold until the batch is cleared, so drift cannot compound past the weekly review window.

### ORCH-CONFORMANCE-1 — Codified architectural commitments are enforced gates, not advisory documents
The architectural commitments the project codifies for its autonomous agents are enforced as gates on changes, not held as advisory documents. A deterministic check (a lint rule, a type-system constraint, an AST scan, an AI reviewer reading against the rules, or a combination of those) is wired into the change pipeline. A change that violates a commitment is rejected, escalated, or carries an explicit waiver naming the commitment, the violation, and the reviewer who waived it. Waivers are recorded in a durable log so periodic human review can sweep them and decide whether the commitment needs refinement.

### ORCH-ENV-GATED-QUALITY-1 — Environment-gated quality — nothing ships unverified
No autonomously-generated code reaches a shared/prod environment without passing the local quality bar (compile + lint clean + tests green); schema changes pass a migration-safety check. Multiple environments (dev/staging/prod) gate changes before they reach users.

### ORCH-NEW-PATH-TESTS-1 — AI-authored code paths carry tests, not just a passing existing suite
A change that adds a new code path carries new tests for that path, not only a green run of the existing test suite. The new test exercises the new branch directly, and a change-time check verifies that the new path is actually reached by the new test (a coverage diff on the introduced lines, a mutation check on the new branch, or equivalent). The rule applies to every change but is load-bearing for AI-authored ones, because agents can write code that compiles and that the existing suite passes while leaving the new branch entirely uncovered.

### ORCH-PREREVIEW-1 — Every change carries an AI reviewer's verdict into the human review queue
Code changes authored by autonomous agents pass through an AI review step. A reviewer agent reads the diff against the project's codified architectural commitments and either approves the change, requests specific changes, or escalates the change as out of scope. The human review queue contains only changes the reviewer agent has cleared or has explicitly flagged for human attention. The reviewer step is wired into the change pipeline (a pre-merge hook, a CI job, a bot on the pull request, or equivalent) such that the change cannot reach the human merge gate without an approval status from the reviewer agent.

### ORCH-REVIEWER-SPLIT-1 — The reviewer agent for any change is distinct from the author agent
The agent that reviews a change is a separate agent from the one that authored it. Author and reviewer differ in some load-bearing configuration: a different prompt, a different principles set, a different model, or a combination of those. The reviewer reads the diff, the change's stated intent, and the codified architectural commitments. The reviewer does not read the author agent's reasoning trace. This separation holds across all orchestrated work regardless of whether author and reviewer share a model family.

### PROC-CITE-CONVENTION-ID-1 — Cite the convention ID in commit messages and PR descriptions
A commit (or PR description) that applies a documented convention cites its ID in the body, for example 'per ENTITIES-4' or 'follows RUST-DOMAIN-2'. The citation lives in the commit body, not the subject line, so the convention reference does not crowd the headline.

### PROC-FEATURE-FLAGS-1 — Feature flags via env var or runtime config; no long-lived feature branches
Incomplete features ship behind a per-environment gate (an env var, a config-service flag, or a runtime configuration entry) checked at runtime via a single helper. The code lives on the trunk but user-visible behavior stays off until the gate is configured to allow it. Long-lived feature branches are not used; work merges incrementally behind a gate instead. When the feature is shipped, the gate and its check are removed in a follow-up cleanup change rather than left behind as permanent infrastructure.

### SPIRIT-DOC-DECISIONS-1 — Document decisions immediately
Every decision (architectural, process, convention, even small) is written to a persistent artifact the moment it is made: a conventions file with a rule ID for code, a dated decision doc for process.

### UI-QUERY-LIBRARY-1 — All UI data fetches go through a query library, never ad-hoc fetch and store
Read paths in components go through a query library (TanStack Query, SWR, dioxus-query, Vue Query, or equivalent for the framework). Mutations explicitly invalidate the cache keys they affect. Ad-hoc fetch-and-store inside each component (manual useState plus useEffect plus fetch) is forbidden. The query library owns request de-duplication, response caching, stale-while-revalidate behavior, refetch on focus or reconnect, and the single point of invalidation.

### UI-UTC-DATES-1 — Render dates through a project-level helper that pins UTC; never format inline
All date and time rendering in the UI goes through a single project-level helper that pins the timezone explicitly (commonly UTC, sometimes a known display-tz). Direct calls to the platform's locale-aware formatting (toLocaleString, strftime with no tz, Intl.DateTimeFormat with no timeZone option) in feature code are forbidden and lint-enforced. The helper is the one place timezone decisions live.

### RUST-DOMAIN-1 — Single domain crate, modules by bounded context
One domain crate. Bounded contexts are internal modules, not separate crates.

### RUST-DOMAIN-2 — Newtype IDs (every ID is a wrapper, never a bare Uuid)
UserId(Uuid), OrganizationId(Uuid); bare Uuid only at persistence and HTTP boundaries.

### RUST-DOMAIN-3 — Newtype validated strings (Email, Url, Slug, …)
Constrained string types are newtypes with a fallible constructor (Email::try_new); the type itself is proof of validation. Free-form text stays as String.

### RUST-DOMAIN-4 — Errors via thiserror enums per crate
Each layer's crate defines its own thiserror enum (DomainError, RepositoryError, ServiceError, …) with explicit From impls at boundaries. anyhow is reserved for application edges (binaries).

### RUST-DOMAIN-6 — Errors are category-scoped, one shared enum per failure category at the crate boundary
Each Rust crate exposes errors organized by failure category at its public boundary, with one shared enum per category. The categories are whatever distinct failure surfaces the crate exposes; examples include persistence and IO failures, input validation, business rule violations, parsing, and networking, but the actual set depends on what the crate does. Variants within an enum enumerate the distinct failure modes for that category. Driver-specific or framework-specific errors map into the appropriate category enum at the boundary; they do not leak through the public API directly. Per-function or per-aggregate error types are not used.

### RUST-DIOXUS-1 — UI crate is organized by file role: views, components, hooks, contexts, services
The UI crate splits source by what a file does, not by what feature it belongs to. Page-level components (one folder per route) live under views. Reusable primitives and composites (Button, Modal, Toast and similar) live under components. Custom hooks live under hooks. Cross-tree context providers live under contexts. Server-call wrappers live under services. Pure helpers (formatting, validation, class composition) live under lib. A central Routable enum lives at the top of the crate. Page-level views compose primitives; primitives never compose views. Per-folder rules (no rsx in lib, no service calls in components, and similar) attach to the folder names, so the role of any file is obvious from its path.

### RUST-DIOXUS-10 — Client gates affordances on capability flags carried by responses; raw permission codes never reach the client
Every server response that drives a gated affordance carries a structured set of named capability flags (typically a can field on the response: org.can.manage, session.can.site_admin, project.can.publish). The UI gates rendering on those flags directly. The UI MUST NOT inspect raw permission codes (string identifiers like the permission scope, or array membership checks against a permissions list on the user object), and MUST NOT re-implement permission policy on the client. The shape of the response object should physically not include a raw-codes field; if it does not exist on the wire, the client cannot read it. The server is the only place authorization decisions are computed; the client only renders the decision.

### RUST-DIOXUS-11 — Single binary serves server-rendered HTML, the client bundle, and server-function endpoints
The Dioxus project enables the framework's fullstack feature so that one server binary handles three concerns in one process. It renders the initial HTML on the server (so public content is indexable and first paint is fast). It serves the client bundle, which hydrates the server-rendered HTML on the client. And it mounts the server-function endpoints (per the server-function rule) and any remaining first-class HTTP endpoints (webhooks, health checks, authentication) on the same router. A separate frontend process running a different framework is forbidden. A client-only single-page application with no server-rendered initial paint is permitted only for fully-behind-login products where indexability has no value.

### RUST-DIOXUS-12 — Icons render as inline SVG from static string constants; no runtime icon-library fetches
Icons live in a single icons module as static SVG string constants. A single Icon component takes a name (an enum variant) and a class string, looks up the corresponding SVG, and renders it inline through the framework's raw-inner-html attribute. Sources for the SVG strings are extracted once from whichever icon set the design system uses, stripped of any framework wrapper, and stored as the raw inner SVG. Runtime fetching of an icon library, icon-font web fonts, and large icon-set crates that pull every icon into the binary are all forbidden.

### RUST-DIOXUS-13 — Forms display errors from the domain newtype constructors; the UI never re-implements validation rules
Form fields bind to signals holding raw user text. On submit (or on blur for live-validation), the form constructs the relevant domain newtype through its fallible constructor and displays whatever validation error the constructor returns. The UI does NOT re-implement validation rules: no inline regex for email format, no max-length check duplicated in the component, no decimal parser repeated per form. The domain newtype is the rule; the UI's job is to call the constructor and render whatever the domain says is wrong. Multi-field forms compose a form-level type that exposes its own fallible constructor in turn.

### RUST-DIOXUS-14 — Common UI elements have one canonical implementation in a primitives layer; views compose primitives
The UI crate has two distinct kinds of components: primitives (Button, Modal, Toast, Card, Input, and similar reusable elements) and views (page-level components that compose primitives to express a feature). Every primitive has exactly one canonical implementation in a shared components directory. Views compose those primitives; views never reimplement a primitive inline or fork one for local convenience. Design-system decisions (color palette, corner radii, type system, focus rings, motion) live in the primitive component, so every view that composes the primitive inherits those decisions for free. A view that needs behavior a primitive does not yet expose extends the primitive's API rather than copying the primitive.

### RUST-DIOXUS-2 — Components are free functions returning Element; multi-prop components derive Props, Clone, and PartialEq
Every component is a free function whose body returns Element, annotated with the component attribute. Single-prop or two-prop components may declare props inline as function arguments. Components with three or more props, or with any non-trivial prop, declare a dedicated props struct that derives the Props, Clone, and PartialEq traits. Struct-based components implementing a component trait, trait-object wrappers, and the legacy Scope-bearing signature from earlier framework versions are forbidden. Untyped value bags as props are forbidden.

### RUST-DIOXUS-3 — Local component state is a Signal, read via call or read, written via set or with_mut
Local component state uses the framework's signal primitive. Display interpolation reads the signal directly via call syntax or the read accessor. Replacing a value uses the set method; in-place mutation of a contained collection uses with_mut. Legacy state hooks from earlier framework versions are forbidden. Threading mutable state through props as a borrowed reference is forbidden. When state is shared across siblings, lift it to a common ancestor; when state is genuinely app-wide, use the cross-tree context pattern rather than passing signals through every layer.

### RUST-DIOXUS-4 — Cross-tree state lives in named per-concern contexts, provided at or near the app root
App-wide state (current user and session, modal stack, consent state, theme) is exposed through the framework's context system. Each concern gets its own context, named for what it is (AuthContext, ModalStackContext, ConsentContext). Providers are mounted at or near the app root. Contexts that own reactive state expose it as a signal so that consumers participate in subscription tracking. A single mega-context that holds every concern at once is forbidden because it forces every reader to re-render on every change, regardless of which concern actually moved.

### RUST-DIOXUS-5 — Side effects run inside use_effect; dependencies auto-track from signal reads in the closure
Side effects (telemetry emission, scroll-to-top on route change, manual DOM measurement, third-party widget initialization) run inside the framework's effect hook. Dependencies are tracked automatically from the signals the closure reads; there is no manual dependency array. Data fetching does NOT belong in an effect; use the resource hook for that. Cleanup runs through the drop implementation of state captured by the closure, the same way the framework cleans up any other state when a tracked signal changes.

### RUST-DIOXUS-6 — Async UI data goes through the resource hook; fire-and-forget work goes through spawn
Two patterns, two helpers. Data that the UI displays (a profile fetched from the server, a list rendered after a query) is acquired through the framework's resource hook. The hook tracks dependencies the same way an effect does: signals read inside the async closure auto-subscribe, and the resource re-fetches when those signals change. The result is a three-state value (loading, success, failure) that the UI matches on directly. Fire-and-forget work that has no UI consumer (analytics, audit log entry, send-and-forget mutation) uses the spawn primitive. The two patterns are not interchangeable: spawning from an effect for data that the UI needs to display is the legacy effect-plus-fetch pattern this rule exists to replace.

### RUST-DIOXUS-7 — Event handlers are move closures in templates; callback props use the framework EventHandler type
Inline event handlers in the template are move closures, because the closure outlives the template expression and must own any state it captures. The framework's signal type is copyable, so capture is cheap. When a component exposes a callback as a prop, the prop is typed as the framework's EventHandler type parameterized by the value the callback delivers; the parent receives it as part of the props derive. The child invokes it through the call method. Bare function-trait closures, boxed function-trait objects, and bare function pointers in props are forbidden because they break the prop derives that the diffing pipeline depends on.

### RUST-DIOXUS-8 — Templates use native control flow; iterated children must carry a stable key
Inside the framework's template macro, conditional rendering uses native if expressions, optional rendering uses native if-let expressions, iteration uses native for expressions, and multi-branch rendering uses native match expressions. Signal and string interpolation happens through curly-brace placeholders in attribute and text positions. Every child produced by a for expression carries a stable key derived from a property of the underlying data, never the loop index. Without a stable key, the framework cannot diff list reorders and every row remounts, destroying child state.

### RUST-DIOXUS-9 — Server-to-client calls use server functions, not a separately maintained HTTP client
When the UI needs data or actions from the server, the framework's server-function macro is used to declare them. The macro generates the client-side call (over the wire, serialized) and the server-side handler from a single function definition. The function body runs server-side and may call the application's service layer directly. A separately maintained REST or RPC client crate that the UI imports is forbidden, because the same route surface would then have to be kept in sync across two boundaries. When the function legitimately needs to talk to a third party (payment provider, email gateway, external API), the call to that third party lives in the server-side body of a server function, never in client-side code.
